In 1910, an educator named Abraham Flexner, who held a bachelor's degree in classics and had never studied medicine, was sent by the Carnegie Foundation to inspect every medical school in the United States and Canada.

He visited 155 of them. His report recommended closing most.

What happened next is the foundation of every health system you know today.

John D. Rockefeller, the founder of Standard Oil and at the time the richest man on Earth, saw an opening. Standard Oil's refining produced vast quantities of coal-tar and petrochemical byproducts. Those byproducts were, conveniently, the raw material from which most early synthetic pharmaceuticals could be made.

Through his General Education Board and the Rockefeller Foundation, Rockefeller directed approximately $50 million, around $10 billion in today's money, into American medical schools. The money came with a condition. The schools had to teach allopathic, drug-based medicine. Anything else was defunded.

Within twenty-five years, more than half of America's 155 medical schools had closed. By 1935, only 66 remained. The schools teaching homeopathy, naturopathy, herbalism, eclectic medicine, and what we would now call nutrition and lifestyle medicine were starved out of existence. Five of America's seven Black medical colleges were closed. Most of the women's medical colleges were closed. The surviving schools all taught one paradigm. They still do.

The petroleum byproduct became the medicine. The medicine became the curriculum. The curriculum became the doctor. The doctor became the prescription pad.

The model that emerged has one structural feature no regulator can legislate away. The pharmaceutical industry earns its revenue when you take a pill. It does not earn revenue when you stop needing the pill. A cure is a one-time transaction. A chronic condition is a subscription.

This is not a conspiracy theory. It is on the income statement. From 2017 to 2024, the average net profit margin of pharmaceutical manufacturers was 23.2%, roughly ten times higher than every other sector of the drug supply chain. The system is performing exactly as designed.

A century after Flexner, the population of the wealthiest country on Earth is fatter, sicker, and on more medication than at any point in human history.

The grass-fed beef, the egg from a hen you can name, the butter, the morning walk, the eight hours of sleep, are all unpatentable.

That is why no oil baron funded a hundred medical schools to teach them.

quoted_tweet.text:
For decades, protein metabolism research suggested that muscle protein synthesis plateaus around 20–25 g protein per meal. That conclusion was based on studies measuring anabolic responses over 4–6 hours following moderate protein doses.

A new controlled trial extended the assessment window to 12 hours and tested doses up to 100 g—revealing that the ceiling effect doesn't exist.

Participants consumed 0 g, 25 g, or 100 g intrinsically labeled milk protein following resistance exercise. Researchers applied continuous isotope tracer infusion and measured muscle protein synthesis across multiple tissue fractions.

Myofibrillar protein synthesis rates increased dose-dependently throughout the entire 12-hour period. The 100 g dose produced 40% higher synthesis rates during hours 4–12 compared to 25 g.

Dietary-protein-derived amino acids continued appearing in circulation for over 12 hours following 100 g protein. By the end of the measurement period, only 53% of ingested amino acids had entered circulation—digestion was still ongoing.

Whole-body protein synthesis increased linearly with protein dose. Amino acid oxidation increased minimally—less than 15% of the increment in protein intake was oxidized. Over 85% was directed toward tissue protein synthesis.

Muscle tissue incorporated 13 g of dietary amino acids over 12 hours following the 100 g dose—13% of total intake. Previous estimates from shorter protocols suggested ~10%.

Exogenous amino acids contributed 27% of myofibrillar protein synthesis following 100 g protein versus only 9% following 25 g. The higher dose didn't just extend the duration of synthesis—it fundamentally shifted the precursor pool composition.

Protein breakdown rates didn't differ between treatments. The anabolic response was driven entirely by increased synthesis, not suppressed breakdown.

Molecular signaling showed a different timeline than metabolic outcomes.

mTOR phosphorylation and p70S6K activation peaked within 4 hours and returned to baseline—regardless of dose. But muscle protein synthesis remained elevated beyond 12 hours when 100 g was consumed.

This dissociation demonstrates that anabolic signaling initiates the response, but sustained amino acid availability—not sustained mTOR activation—drives prolonged synthesis.

Muscle free leucine concentrations remained elevated throughout the 12-hour period following 100 g protein. Other amino acids didn't accumulate in the muscle free pool.

Leucine's selective retention supports its dual role: signaling molecule during initial mTOR activation and metabolic substrate during prolonged synthesis.

Current dietary guidelines recommend distributing protein evenly across meals to maximize the anabolic response at each feeding opportunity. That advice assumes the response is transient and saturable.

But if a large protein dose produces sustained anabolism lasting well beyond 12 hours, frequent feeding becomes less critical for optimizing muscle protein synthesis.

Time-restricted feeding protocols—which compress food intake into narrow windows—may not compromise anabolism if sufficient protein is consumed during the feeding period.

The postprandial state may extend far beyond the feeding window when large protein doses are consumed.

The study also challenges assumptions about mTOR, autophagy, and protein turnover.

Ingesting 100 g protein sustained anabolism without suppressing whole-body protein breakdown or muscle autophagy markers. This suggests prolonged anabolism doesn't require a subsequent fasted state to allow protein clearance.

Limitations: The 12-hour measurement window wasn't sufficient to capture the full response to 100 g protein. Digestion and absorption were ongoing when the protocol ended. The magnitude and duration of the response are likely underestimated.

The study was conducted in young men following resistance exercise. Anabolic sensitivity to feeding is lower in sedentary and older populations, so ceiling effects may exist in those groups despite not appearing here.

But the mechanistic data provide clarity on postprandial protein handling at a systems level.

Protein digestion is prolonged. Amino acid absorption is sustained. Exogenous amino acid flux into circulation continues for >12 hours. Tissue uptake doesn't plateau. Incorporation into muscle and plasma protein increases proportionally to dose.

The anabolic response to protein ingestion has no upper limit within the range tested (0–100 g).

Meal size and meal frequency may be more flexible variables than current guidelines suggest. Larger, less frequent meals produce metabolic outcomes comparable to—or exceeding—smaller, more frequent feeding.

Here's our full review: https://t.co/hI5AUtYjT8

Whole-body protein synthesis increased linearly with protein dose. Amino acid oxidation increased minimally—less than 15% of the increment in protein intake was oxidized. Over 85% was directed toward tissue protein synthesis.

Molecular signaling showed a different timeline than metabolic outcomes.

mTOR phosphorylation and p70S6K activation peaked within 4 hours and returned to baseline—regardless of dose. But muscle protein synthesis remained elevated beyond 12 hours when 100 g was consumed.

Muscle free leucine concentrations remained elevated throughout the 12-hour period following 100 g protein. Other amino acids didn't accumulate in the muscle free pool.

Protein digestion is prolonged. Amino acid absorption is sustained. Exogenous amino acid flux into circulation continues for >12 hours. Tissue uptake doesn't plateau. Incorporation into muscle and plasma protein increases proportionally to dose.
